svg.text: The relationship decision tree
For every pair of entities, ask "how many of A relate to how many of B?" — then apply the matching pattern.
1 · One-to-Many (1:N)
Cue: "A has many B, but each B belongs to one A"
Fix: put a foreign key on the "many" side.
artist → albums · user → posts · recipe → steps
albums.artist_id → artists.artist_id (FK lives on albums)
2 · Many-to-Many (M:N)
Cue: "A has many B AND B has many A"
Fix: a junction table with two FKs (+ unique constraint).
playlist↔song · user↔favorite · student↔class
playlist_songs(playlist_id, song_id, sort_order) unique(playlist_id,song_id)
3 · Self-Referencing (the threaded / hierarchy case)
Cue: "B can relate to other B" — replies, org charts, categories
Fix: a nullable FK on the table pointing back at itself.
comment → parent comment · employee → manager
comments.parent_comment_id → comments.comment_id (null = top level)
4 · Attributes ON the relationship
Cue: a fact describes the PAIRING, not either entity
Fix: put those columns on the junction table.
"2 cups of flour" · "joined this subreddit as mod on date"
recipe_ingredients(recipe_id, ingredient_id, quantity, unit)
The one question that unlocks every relationship:
"How many of A relate to how many of B?" — the answer points you straight at pattern 1, 2, 3, or 4.
The "what happens when..." follow-ups they always throw (pre-empt them)
• "...lots of child items (steps, ingredients)?" → separate 1:N table + explicit sort_order column (insertion order is not guaranteed).
• "...a user favorites / likes / follows?" → M:N junction table + unique constraint to stop duplicates.
• "...replies to replies (nesting)?" → self-referencing parent_id (adjacency list); name recursive CTE for reads; closure table if very deep.
• "...this gets huge / slow?" → index the FKs, cache counts, denormalize for reads, partition by date, feed a star schema for analytics.
• "...the same thing can be voted/tagged in multiple places?" → polymorphic association (type + id), and name the tradeoff vs separate tables.
